theory.concept:
Real queries often need more than two tables — you can chain multiple JOINs in a single query to combine data across several related tables.

- Each additional JOIN needs its own ON clause connecting it to a table already in the query
- The order of JOINs generally doesn't affect the result, only readability — but it's common to join in an order that follows the logical relationship between tables
- Aliases become essential here — with three or more tables, unaliased queries get hard to read fast
- You can mix join types — e.g. an INNER JOIN followed by a LEFT JOIN — depending on which relationships must match and which are optional

example.query:
-- 1. Three tables joined together
SELECT s.name, e.course, d.department_name FROM Students AS s
JOIN Enrollments AS e ON s.id = e.student_id
JOIN Departments AS d ON s.department_id = d.id;

shared_table.name: Students
shared_table.insert_sql:
INSERT INTO Students (id, name, department_id) VALUES
(1, 'Aditya', 1),
(2, 'Priya', 2),
(3, 'Rohan', 1),
(4, 'Sneha', 3),
(5, 'Karan', 2);

shared_table.name: Enrollments
shared_table.insert_sql:
INSERT INTO Enrollments (id, student_id, course) VALUES
(1, 1, 'DBMS'),
(2, 2, 'Networks'),
(3, 3, 'DBMS'),
(4, 5, 'OS');

shared_table.name: Departments
shared_table.insert_sql:
INSERT INTO Departments (id, department_name) VALUES
(1, 'Computer Science'),
(2, 'Electronics'),
(3, 'Mechanical');

questions.prompt: List each enrolled student's name, course, and department name.
Count how many enrolled students belong to each department.
List every student's name and department, including students with no enrollment.
Find department names where at least one enrolled student is taking 'DBMS'.
